coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of catalytically active carbon quantum dots and its application for colorimetric detection of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Qingmei
Chen, Yuye
Su, Anmei
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from wood soot, and the CQDs could catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to form oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with an absorption peak at 652 nm. It was found that the introduction of glutathione (GSH) could cause the reduction of ox-TMB, resulting in a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm. Based on this, a highly sensitive colorimetric sensor for detection of GSH was fabricated. The influences of various experimental variables, including pH, temperature, and the concentrations of H2O2, CQDs and TMB, on the analytical performance of the method were systematically investigated. Under the conditions of pH 3.5, temperature 35 °C, H2O2 1.0 mM, CQDs 2.5 μg/mL and TMB 0.5 mM, the response was linearly proportional to the concentration of GSH within the range of 0.05 to 20 μM with a low detect limit (3 σ/k) of 0.016 μM. The method was successfully applied to determination of GSH in human serum samples with the recovery of 95.7%–103.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Peroxidase-like activity
3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine
Determination
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of the sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the colorimetric method for GSH detection.
The obtained CQDs of TEM image (A), high-resolution image of one particle (B), size distribution (C), and FTIR spectra (D).
UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB in (a) absence and (b) presence of GSH. Inset shows the corresponding images.

(A). UV–vis absorption spectra of the proposed method in the absence and presence of different concentrations of GSH. (B) Plots of ΔA with the different concentrations of GSH. The inset shows the corresponding photographic images. From left to right; the concentration of GSH is 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20 μM, respectively.

Selectivity of the proposed method for GSH against other amino acids. The concentration of GSH, Cys and Hcy are 1.0 mM, the other amino acids concentrations are 100 mM, respectively. The inset shows the corresponding photographic images.
Performance comparison for the proposed method with other detection methods of GSH.
Application of the proposed method for determination of GSH in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of catalytically active carbon quantum dots and its application for colorimetric detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from wood soot, and the CQDs could catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to form oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with an absorption peak at 652 nm. It was found that the introduction of glutathione (GSH) could cause the reduction of ox-TMB, resulting in a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm. Based on this, a highly sensitive colorimetric sensor for detection of GSH was fabricated. The influences of various experimental variables, including pH, temperature, and the concentrations of H2O2, CQDs and TMB, on the analytical performance of the method were systematically investigated. Under the conditions of pH 3.5, temperature 35 °C, H2O2 1.0 mM, CQDs 2.5 μg/mL and TMB 0.5 mM, the response was linearly proportional to the concentration of GSH within the range of 0.05 to 20 μM with a low detect limit (3 σ/k) of 0.016 μM. The method was successfully applied to determination of GSH in human serum samples with the recovery of 95.7%–103.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (γ-glutamyl–cysteinyl–glycine, GSH) plays an essential role in maintaining the function of the normal immune system. It has a hand in bio-transformation, which turns harmful toxins in the body into harmless substances and excreted out of the body [1–4]. Studies have shown that the content of GSH is associated with many diseases, such as cancer, heart problems, aids and neurodegenerative disorders [5–10]. Therefore, it is of great significance to determine the content of glutathione in body fluids. Up to now, different analytical methods such as high performance liquid chromatography [11–13], mass spectrometry [14], fluorescence [15,16], electrochemistry [17], and calorimetric method [18,19] have been used for the determination of glutathione. Among all existing methods, colorimetric analytic approach would be a more desirable method due to the characteristics of low cost, simplicity and practicality [20]. In addition to no requirement of sophisticated instrumentation, the colorimetric method can also transform the detection events into color changes, which is suitable for naked eye observation [21].

Because of their superior optical properties, good chemical stability, low toxicity and excellent bio-compatibility [22,23], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in many fields, such as fluorescent probe [24,25], biological imaging [26,27] and photo-catalysis [28]. Since Shi [29] reported that CQDs could be used as peroxidase mimetics, which opened a new field for the application of CQDs [30,31]. In this work, CQDs were prepared from wood soot, and the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs for the application of GSH detection was explored based on CQDs –H2O2 –TMB system. Previous reports [29,31,32] have shown that CQDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to generate oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with an absorption peak at 652 nm. It is well known that GSH could cause the reduction of ox-TMB [33] which resulted in the color fading and a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm. Based on these facts, a rapid and simple colorimetric sensor for determination of GSH was fabricated, and satisfactory results were achieved when the sensor was applied for the detection of GSH in human serum samples.

TMB and GSH were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide, acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and other regents were obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. Glycine (Gly), histidine (His), threonine (Thr), arginine (Arg), serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), and other kinds of amino acids used in this wok were purchased from Da Mao Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). Serum samples were obtained from a local hospital. All of these reagents were analytical reagent grade and used as received. Doubly deionized water (DDW) was used throughout.

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV–4802 spectrophotometer (Unico, Shanghai, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) image was taken at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. FTIR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd. Madison, WI, USA).

The wood soot was taken from the bottom of a pot, and the CQDs were prepared according to the literature [34]. Briefly, after being washed with acetone and dried at 110 °C, 0.5 g of wood soot was added into 150 mL of HNO3 (5 M), and the mixture was refluxed at 140 °C for 12 h. The CQDs were purified by neutralizing the resulting black solution with sodium carbonate, and then dialyzed against deionized water for 2 days. Acetone was added to the purified solution and the CQDs were collected by centrifugation at 16,000 rpm for 15 min. The solid was dried in vacuum oven at 40 °C, dispersed in DDW, and then ultra-filtered to remove large particles. The brown CQDs solution obtained was stored at 4 °C.

A stock solution of 10 mM GSH was prepared in DDW and various concentrations of GSH were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. The determination of GSH was performed as follows: Firstly, 7.4 mL of NaAc-HAc buffer solutions (pH = 3.5), 0.5 mL of 50 μg/mL CQDs, 1.0 mL of 10 mM H2O2, 1.0 mL of 5 mM TMB and 100 μL of aqueous solution of GSH at various concentrations were sequentially added to a series of 10 mL colorimetric tubes. Then the mixture solution was thoroughly mixed and incubated at 35 °C water bath for 10 min. Finally, the corresponding absorbance values were recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy.

For serum sample preparation, various amounts of GSH were spiked to the 1.0 mL of serum sample and then 10% trichloroacetic acid (0.5 mL) was added to remove serum protein. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm and the supernatant was taken carefully. Finally, the samples detection was carried out using above procedure.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out to study the size and morphology of CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 1 A, the obtained particles are spherical in shape and show good homogeneity in the particle size distribution. The interlayer spacing is d(002) = 0.35 nm (obtained from high-resolution image, shown as the inset in Fig. 1B). The statistics shows that the diameters of CQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 1.3–3.7 nm and average diameter was calculated to be 2.3 nm (Fig. 1C).

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CQDs is recorded in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 3378 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of OH, respectively. The peaks at 1130 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO, respectively. These peaks are consistent with the literature [35,36] and confirm the water solubility of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
Previous researches [29,31,32] have shown that CQDs worked as peroxidase-like agent. CQDs could catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrate (TMB) in the presence of H2O2, leading to a colored product (ox-TMB). As illustrated in Scheme 1 , CQDs catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to ox-TMB (equ.1) along with a blue color and a strong absorption peak centered at 652 nm (Fig. 2 a). However, the introduction of GSH to the CQDs-H2O2-TMB system resulted in the blue color fading and a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 2b). Further investigation showed that the process is irreversible (Fig. S1). This may be attributed to the fact that the GSH cause the reduction of ox-TMB (equ.2) [33]. The absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm decreases with the addition of GSH. Therefore, we could detect GSH by the variation of absorbance.
(1)
H 2
O 2
+ T M B
⟶
C Q D s
H 2
O + o x − T M B
(2)
o x − T M B + 2 G S H → T M B + G S S G + 2
H +

Several experimental conditions that may affect the sensitivity were optimized prior to the application, including pH, temperature, and the concentrations of CQDs, H2O2 and TMB. ΔA was used as optimization criterion for the detection conditions, ΔA = A 0 − A, where A 0 and A are the absorbance at 652 nm in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively.

pH is a crucial factor to affect the detection because GSH has a variety of functional groups such as carbonyl, amine and thiol. Therefore, the effect of pH was first investigated by using 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer solution with different pH values (2.5–6.0).The results (Fig. S2) indicated that the ΔA was much higher in weakly acidic solutions than that in strong acidic and near neutral solutions, suggesting that the oxidation reaction of TMB occurred under weakly acidic conditions, which is similar to those of other nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity [37,38]. Thus, pH 3.5 was chosen for the further experiments.

Temperature played an important role in most reactions, the temperature being too low or too high was not suitable for the catalytic reaction. Therefore, the effect of temperature ranging from 25 to 50 °C on ΔA was investigated and shown in Fig. S3. The ΔA increased with the increasing temperature in the range from 25 to 35 °C. Further increased in temperature resulted in the decrease of ΔA. Accordingly, 35 °C was selected as the optimal incubation temperature.

In this study, H2O2 as oxidant and CQDs as catalyst were used to oxidize TMB to produce color reaction, and the concentrations of H2O2 and CQDs were important factors that influenced the change in color and ΔA value. Meanwhile TMB served as a substrate for color reaction, and its concentration on the sensitivity of the detection system also had a great influence. Therefore, the effect of concentrations on ΔA was studied, and the results were displayed in Fig.S4-6. It can be obviously seen that the value of ΔA increased first and then decreased with the increase of concentration (H2O2, CQDs and TMB). The maximum ΔA was obtained when the H2O2 concentration was 1.0 mM, CQDs concentration was 2.5μg/mL and TMB concentration was 0.5 mM. Therefore, 1.0 mM of H2O2, 2.5μg/mL of CQDs and 0.5 mM of TMB were chosen for the next experiments.

Under the optimized conditions, the effect of GSH concentrations on absorption spectra was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with the increase of GSH concentration, the absorbance at 652 nm decreased gradually and tended to disappear when the concentration of GSH reached 20 μM. Furthermore, the ΔA exhibited a good linear relationship when the concentration of GSH ranged from 0.05 to 20 μM (Fig. 3B), and the regression equation is ΔA = 0.0453 + 0.0332C (GSH, μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9932. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.016 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is lower than some previous reports [33,40,41]. More importantly, from the photographs (Fig. 3B inset), the corresponding color of the solution gradually changed from dark blue to light blue, and the concentration of 20 μM GSH can be clearly observed by naked eye. In addition, we compared the characteristics of the proposed method with other previous works reported elsewhere. It can be seen from Table 1 , the sensitivity of our method is higher than or comparable to them. Although some other methods are higher than ours, it is not as simple and low cost as our approach. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for five replicate determinations was 2.9% for 10.0 μM GSH, indicating acceptable reproducibility and precision of the method.

The selectivity of this colorimetric method was evaluated (Fig. 4 ). Several kinds of amino acids, including glycine (Gly), histidine (His), threonine (Thr), arginine (Arg), serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glc), cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) were examined under the optimum conditions. It is clearly seen that ΔA in the presence of GSH is strikingly larger than that of the potential interferences. Though Cys and Hcy show a little high absorbance change, they are much lower compared with GSH. These results indicated that the proposed colorimetric method had a high selectivity toward GSH.

Evaluating the feasibility of the method for detection in real samples was conducted to the determination of GSH in human serum samples. The results were presented in Table 2 . It can be seen that the measured values were consistent with the spiked values for the concentration of GSH. The recoveries of GSH in the samples range from 95.7% to 103.6% with RSD between 0.9% and 6.7%, which is acceptable for quantitative detection performed in complex real samples. These results indicated that the proposed method has great potential for the determination of GSH in real samples.

In summary, we reported a new colorimetric sensing method to detect GSH based on CQDs-H2O2-TMB system. It was believed that CQDs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 in acidic media to generate oxidized TMB with an absorption peak at 652 nm. The introduction of GSH to above system resulted in the color fading and a decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm. Under the optimized conditions, a good linear relationship between ΔA and GSH concentration in the range of 0.05–20 μM was achieved with detection limit of 0.016 μM. More importantly, the proposed method exhibited its potential application in the detection of GSH in human serum sample. CQDs as peroxidase mimics showed some advantages over natural enzymes such as easy preparation, low cost, good water solubility and stable catalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2014GXNSFAA118328), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-2) and the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2016K003)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of glutathione based on Ag+ - 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)

Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
A dual-channel fluorescent chemosensor for discriminative detection of glutathione based on functionalized carbon quantum dots
A near-infrared colorimetric fluorescent chemodosimeter for the detection of glutathione in living cells
The antioxidant role of selenium and seleno-compounds
Determination of cellular glutathione: glutathione disulfide ratio in prostate cancer cells by high performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection
3-iodoacetylaminobenzanthrone as a fluorescent derivatizing reagent for thiols in high-performance liquid chromatography
Pre-column derivatization high-performance liquid chromatographic method for determination of cysteine, cysteinyl-glycine, homocysteine and glutathione in plasma and cell extracts
Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry of low-molecular-mass S-nitroso compounds and their thiols
BODIPY-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
A thiol specific fluorescent probe and its application for bioimaging
Electrochemical determination of free and total glutathione in human saliva samples
A dispersion-dominated chromogenic strategy for colorimetric sensing of glutathione at the nanomolar level using gold nanoparticles
A novel colorimetric determination of reduced glutathione in A549 cells based on Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics
Colorimetric detection of glucose based on ficin with peroxidase-like activity
A simple label free colorimetric method for glyphosate detection based on the inhibition of peroxidase-like activity of Cu(Ⅱ)
Carbon quantum dots: a safe tool to learn about quantum phenomenon in nanomaterials
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from polyvinyl pyrrolidone and their multicolor cell imaging
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for “off-on” detection of mercury ions, L-cysteine and iodide ions
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
High-quality carbon dots: synthesis, peroxidase-like activity and their application in the detection of H2O2, Ag+ and Fe3+
Synthesis of catalytically active multielement-doped carbon dots and application for colorimetric detection of glucose
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase nanozymes for biosensing
Colorimetric detection of glutathione in human blood serum based on the reduction of oxidized TMB
Study on the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots
Carbon dots as nontoxic and High-performance fluorescence imaging agents
Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection
Application of photocatalytic cadmium sulfide nanoparticles to detection of enzymatic activities of glucose oxidase and glutathione reductase using oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine
FePt-Au ternary metallic nanoparticles with the enhanced peroxidase-like activity for ultrafast colorimetric detection of H2O2
A sensitive polymer dots-manganese dioxide fluorescent nanosensor for “turn-on” detection of glutathione in human serum
Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of glutathione based on Ag+ - 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)
Sensitive and selective detection of biothiols based on target-induced agglomeration of silvernanoclusters
Turn-on persistent luminescence probe based on graphitic carbon nitride for imaging detection of biothiols in biological fluids
Carbon dots based photoelectrochemical sensors for ultrasensitive detection of glutathione and its applications in probing of myocardial infarction
In situ synthesis of self-assembled three-dimensional graphene–magnetic palladium nanohybrids with dual-enzyme activity through one-pot strategy and Its application in glucose probe

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qingmei Zhong received her BS degree from Guangxi University in 2016, China. Currently, she is studying for master degree under the supervision of Prof. Yilin Wang in Guangxi University. Her research interests focus mainly on the development of colorimetric sensors.

Yuye Chen received her BS degree from Guangxi University in 2017, China. Currently, she is studying for master degree under the supervision of Prof. Yilin Wang in Guangxi University. Her research interests focus mainly on the application of carbon nanomaterials in detection.

Anmei Su received her BS degree from Guangxi University in 2015, China. Currently, she is studying for master degree under the supervision of Prof. Yilin Wang in Guangxi University. Her research interests focus mainly on the application of carbon nanomaterials.

Yilin Wang obtained his Ph.D. degree in 2010 from Guangxi University, China. Currently, he is a Professor of College of Chemistry, Guangxi University. His recent research focuses on Chemo-and Bio-Sensing.
